content:
# Velocity Estimation from Surface-Consistent Statics

**Summary**: The thesis proposes a new method for estimating accurate subsurface NMO velocities from residual static corrections in seismic data. The methodology was validated using synthetic models (flat and dipping) and real seismic datasets, demonstrating that the technique is independent of initial velocity errors and dip angles.

---

## Research Problem

Standard velocity analysis methods (like the semblance method) are often hampered by poor signal-to-noise ratios, complex geology, and specifically, the impact of near-surface irregularities (statics). This study addresses the problem of estimating accurate NMO velocities when initial velocity models are imperfect or when statics are poorly estimated.

## Objectives

- Conceptualize and demonstrate a novel approach to velocity estimation.
- Derive a new relation that inverts residual statics due to imperfect NMO analysis to find the correct NMO velocity.
- Evaluate the performance of this technique relative to conventional methods using synthetic and real seismic datasets.
- Propose a new approach for estimating subsurface velocity from residual static corrections.
- Demonstrate the methodology using synthetic data with high and low initial NMO velocities.
- Validate the method on a dipping reflector model.

## Methods

The methodology involves analyzing the relationship between surface-consistent statics and NMO correction. The total static shift (T_ijk) is decomposed into source, receiver, structural, and NMO components. By isolating the NMO term (M), which represents the discrepancy caused by an incorrect initial velocity, a new mathematical model is derived to calculate the correct velocity (Vc). This approach was tested on three datasets: a 1D horizontal layer model with a sand dune (causing significant statics), a dipping reflector model, and a real 2D seismic dataset from southeast Texas.

## Data and Materials

Three distinct datasets were used: 1) A 2D elastic synthetic dataset featuring a 5 km long profile with a sand dune; 2) A 2D acoustic synthetic dataset of a dipping reflector model (6 km length, 2 km depth); 3) A real 2D seismic land data set from southeast Texas, USA, consisting of 18 shot records and 594 traces.

## Key Findings

- The proposed method for estimating velocity from residual statics produced an NMO velocity within 2.5% of the 'true' model value in a sand dune scenario. ([[2023-muhammad|Muhammad, 2023, p. 15]])
- In the dipping reflector model, the technique yielded an NMO velocity within 1% error of the true velocity of the medium. ([[2023-muhammad|Muhammad, 2023, p. 16]])
- On real data from southeast Texas, the method estimated the correct NMO velocity with only 0.6% error when applied to a dataset where an intentionally incorrect velocity was used for initial correction. ([[2023-muhammad|Muhammad, 2023, p. 16]])
- The technique is independent of the initial (incorrect) velocity value used during the first pass of NMO correction, making it robust against both over-corrected and under-corrected data. ([[2023-muhammad|Muhammad, 2023, p. 16]])
- Errors in static corrections can lead to up to a 35% error in estimated velocity if not properly accounted for. ([[2023-muhammad|Muhammad, 2023, p. 18]])
- The method successfully accounts for dipping reflectors by incorporating the cosine of the dip angle into the derivation, showing it is applicable to non-horizontal geometries. ([[2023-muhammad|Muhammad, 2023, p. 50]])
- In the flat synthetic model, the estimated velocity (Vc) ranged from 2700 m/s to 3200 m/s with a mean of 2924 m/s, representing only a 2.5% error from the correct NMO velocity of 3000 m/s. ([[2023-muhammad|Muhammad, 2023, p. 69]])
- In the dipping model (CMP 150), the mean estimated Vc was 2763.79 m/s, which is approximately 1.24% away from the apparent NMO velocity value of 2730 m/s. ([[2023-muhammad|Muhammad, 2023, p. 77]])
- In the real data (CDP 225), the estimated Vc values ranged from 9580 ft/s to 9671 ft/s with a mean of 9635 ft/s, within 1% of the true Vc value of 9581 ft/s. ([[2023-muhammad|Muhammad, 2023, p. 80]])
- The method was shown to be independent of the initial (incorrect) NMO velocity applied and independent of the dip angle. ([[2023-muhammad|Muhammad, 2023, p. 75]])
- The accuracy of the method remained consistent even with varying levels of statics correction (0%, 20%, and 50%), showing a mean error of only 2.5% in the flat model. ([[2023-muhammad|Muhammad, 2023, p. 73]])

## Limitations

The accuracy of the technique is dependent on the quality of the static corrections; errors in statics can lead up to a 35% error in estimated velocity.

## Recommendations and Future Work

- Use an auto-picking algorithm to pick Tijk times at all offsets after removing source and receiver statics.
- Test the methodology in more structurally complex environments beyond simple geology.
- Test the robustness of the model using layers with steeper dips than the 24 degrees tested.
